focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

By the time Junie dragged the third table into place, the supper crowd had gone and Ferrymouth had dropped into its late noises: hulls knocking pilings, a bell from the upriver ferry, gulls disputing over fish skin in the alley, men on the slip laughing too hard because they were too tired to do it softly. The kitchen still held the whole day in its walls. Heat came off the digester stove in wet breaths, and the room smelled of eel broth, pepper vinegar, lamp smoke, yeast, and river mud. Silas had changed his shirt once and then given up on cleanliness as a profession.

Junie jammed a folded rag under a short table leg and looked at him over the stockpot. Flour lived in the old split of her left eyebrow again, making the scar look chalked.

"If you stand there like a hired mourner," she said, "I'll put you by the stove and let everybody sweat at you. Cut the bread. Thick. Nobody trusts a thin slice."

His right wrist was mostly good for hauling, chopping, and being sworn at. It still did not forgive elegance. He took the knife and started through the rye loaves anyway, leaning his weight behind the blade when the ache climbed.

"I wasn't planning a speech," he said.

"You plan them by breathing. Cut."

Otis, missing one front tooth and proud of the vacancy, sorted mismatched spoons by size because he had decided that honest people ought to get the heavier ones. Every few minutes he tested a word through the gap in his mouth to hear what the air did with it. Tonight he had chosen coalition, which came out with a pleased whistle.

Junie caught him at it. "If you whistle that at the table, I'll make you scrub the fish board."

"It whistles by itself," Otis said.

"Then let it do it quietly."

Silas watched his sister move through the room with the steady brut...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...erglow to hide in. The room still smelled of labor. Dawn had not yet broken. The city outside still wanted one name clean enough to own.

"Why me?" he asked quietly. "For this, I mean. Not the other part."

Inez glanced at the ledger, then back at him. "The other part is easier."

The page edge bit a white line into his thumb. He eased his grip and dragged in a slower breath.

She went on. "You know how words travel. Better than anybody I trust less. You also know now what they cost when they travel wrong. That may make you useful. Or it may make you a worse disaster than before. I haven't decided which. I decided only that I would rather choose my risk awake."

Silas closed the ledger with both hands and immediately gave it back.

"That's fair," he said.

"Yes." She took it, retied the blue cord, and slid the packet back behind the chimney stack. "Don't make me stupid for having wanted you."

He stood with his thighs aching and her bite still damp through his shirt at the shoulder. "I won't."

Inez gave him a look that said promises were cheap even now, maybe especially now. Then she rose, gathered her fallen scarf, and moved to unbar the door.

When she lifted the wood free, the first thin gray of morning was leaking into the outer walk. Somewhere beyond the grow houses a ferry horn sounded over the cut, tired and long. Silas followed her out carrying nothing in his hands. Moss and lamp oil stayed on his fingers, and the line from the ledger would not let go of him: mothers in drain, do not carry to market. At the bend in the walk he looked back. Inez was already half inside the propagation shed again, one hand on the bar, the warm dark and the hidden oilskin behind her. Whether he deserved it or not, noon had already fixed him back under that lamp with the book open.
